refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-001 (Refined): The NGHE solution shall enable the operator (on-site or remote) to execute earthmoving/material handling tasks such that the cumulative productivity, measured in m³/h or ton/h over a representative evaluation period, meets or exceeds the customer-defined productivity_target, while maintaining compliance with worksite safety plans and operational policies.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to operator capability, task execution, and safety compliance. Refinement clarifies that productivity is a function of operator effectiveness (whether on-site or tele-operated), task execution fidelity, and safety-compliant operation. This frames productivity as a system-level emergent property dependent on multiple subsystems.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct representative operations trials over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 40 operating hours). Log cumulative material moved (m³ or ton), operational time, safety incidents, and task completion rate. Compare against productivity_target. Verify that no safety plan violations occurred during measurement.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-001.1: The vehicle shall execute commanded tasks (dig, load, transport, dump) with sufficient speed and precision to achieve target cycle times.
CR-NGHE-001.2: The operator interface (on-site or remote) shall provide sufficient situational awareness and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution.
CR-NGHE-001.3: The NGHE solution shall enforce safety constraints (geofencing, speed limits, collision avoidance) that do not degrade productivity below the target.
CR-NGHE-001.4: The teleoperation system (if deployed) shall maintain communication link quality and latency sufficient to sustain operator control fidelity and task execution rate.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Maximize vehicle hydraulic power and bucket capacity; optimize operator cabin ergonomics and visibility; implement predictive maintenance to minimize downtime.

DP-B: Integrate real-time task planning and autonomous task execution modules; provide operator with high-fidelity telemetry and predictive task completion estimates; enforce safety constraints via software governors.
DP-C: Combine vehicle performance optimization (DP-A) with intelligent task orchestration (DP-B) and safety-aware scheduling; use telemetry feedback to adapt task execution in real-time.

DP-D: Implement a modular architecture where vehicle capability, operator interface, teleoperation link, and safety services are independently optimized and integrated via standardized interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a modular architecture where vehicle capability, operator interface, teleoperation link, and safety services are independently optimized and integrated via standardized interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). This DP enables productivity through decoupled optimization of each subsystem while maintaining safety compliance via explicit safety service integration.

potential_sources: Telemetry interface must carry sufficient fidelity (position, load, fuel, health) to enable both operator decision-making and safety service validation.
Tasking interface must support both operator-initiated and autonomous task commands without ambiguity.
SafetyAlert interface must not introduce latency that degrades operator responsiveness.
Network Link quality directly impacts teleoperation fidelity; degradation couples to operator interface effectiveness.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is modular by design but introduces interface coupling. Coupling is manageable if interface contracts are rigorous and latency budgets are enforced. Recommend interface specification review at next level.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Standardized interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) can be defined with sufficient bandwidth and latency guarantees to support both on-site and remote operation.
Risk: If interface latency exceeds operator reaction time budget, teleoperation productivity will degrade below target.
Risk: If safety service integration introduces false-positive alerts, operator will override safety constraints, negating safety compliance.

Assumption: Vehicle hardware (hydraulics, bucket, drivetrain) can achieve target cycle times without modification; productivity is primarily a function of operator effectiveness and task orchestration.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify interface latency and bandwidth requirements as part of DP specification. Define operator reaction time budget and map to Network Link and SafetyAlert latency constraints. Specify SafetyAlert false-positive rate acceptance criteria.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level integration module that orchestrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) and ensures that productivity targets are achieved while safety compliance is maintained. The NGHE Solution module is responsible for system-level performance monitoring, task scheduling, and safety policy enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits real-time vehicle state (position, orientation, load, fuel, hydraulic pressure, health diagnostics) to operator interface and safety services. Frequency: ≥10 Hz for on-site, ≥5 Hz for teleoperation (latency budget TBD).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives task commands (dig, load, transport, dump, emergency stop) from operator or autonomous planner. Commands include target location, task parameters, and priority. Latency: <100 ms for on-site, <500 ms for teleoperation (TBD).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services (geofencing, speed limits, collision warnings). NGHE Solution reports safety-relevant events (constraint violations, near-miss, emergency stop) back to Safety Services. Latency: <50 ms (safety-critical).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link for teleoperation connectivity (if deployed). Carries Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert over wireless or wired network. Availability: ≥99% during operation (TBD). Latency: <500 ms round-trip (TBD).

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level container for CR-NGHE-001. It owns the productivity KPI and orchestrates all subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only interface contracts leak to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Vehicle module owns hardware execution (hydraulics, bucket, drivetrain) but does not own operator interface, teleoperation, or safety policy. Productivity is emergent from Vehicle + Operator + Safety, so containment is partial. High diffusion: productivity depends on external operator and safety services.

Teleoperation module owns remote operator interface and network link but does not own vehicle hardware or safety services. Productivity depends on teleoperation link quality, but is not solely determined by it. High diffusion: productivity is a system-level property.

Safety Services owns safety constraints and alerts but does not own vehicle execution or operator interface. Safety Services enables productivity by preventing unsafe operations, but does not directly drive productivity. High diffusion: safety is a constraint, not a driver of productivity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: CR-NGHE-001 (Productivity KPI) is best owned by NGHE Solution because productivity is an emergent system-level property that depends on the integration of vehicle capability, operator effectiveness, and safety compliance. No single subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can independently achieve the productivity target; the NGHE Solution module must orchestrate all three and monitor the aggregate productivity metric.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution module is too abstract or lacks concrete ownership of task scheduling and performance monitoring, productivity accountability may diffuse to Vehicle or Teleoperation teams.

Risk: If interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are not rigorously specified, subsystems may optimize locally (e.g., Vehicle maximizes speed, Safety Services maximizes caution) at the expense of system-level productivity.
Risk: If NGHE Solution does not own the productivity measurement and reporting mechanism, the KPI may not be verifiable or actionable.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define NGHE Solution module responsibilities explicitly: (1) task scheduling and sequencing, (2) real-time telemetry aggregation and operator feedback, (3) safety constraint enforcement and override logic, (4) productivity metric calculation and reporting. Assign a single owner (e.g., System Integration team) to NGHE Solution module. Specify interface contracts with latency, bandwidth, and reliability requirements.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (modular architecture with standardized interfaces) directly addresses the refined FR by enabling independent optimization of vehicle, operator interface, and safety services while maintaining system-level productivity and safety compliance. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific hardware or algorithms) and is scalable to both on-site and remote operation. No coupling between FR and DP; the mapping is sound.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If interface contracts are not enforced, Vehicle team may optimize for speed without regard to operator control fidelity, causing productivity to degrade in practice.

Diffusion Risk 2: If Safety Services operates independently without real-time feedback from Vehicle and Operator, safety alerts may be delayed or incorrect, reducing operator trust and causing override behavior.
Diffusion Risk 3: If Teleoperation module is not tightly integrated with NGHE Solution, network latency may not be managed end-to-end, causing operator responsiveness to degrade.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution module is the correct container for CR-NGHE-001 because it owns the system-level productivity metric and orchestrates all subsystems via standardized interfaces. Containment is strong (95/100) because the module boundary aligns with the FR scope. Diffusion is minimal (5/100) because interface contracts are explicit and owned by NGHE Solution.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-001 is feasible via representative operations trials. NGHE Solution module must own the productivity measurement mechanism (telemetry logging, task completion tracking, cycle time analysis). Verification requires instrumentation of Telemetry interface to capture vehicle state, task commands, and safety events. Acceptance criteria: productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period, zero safety plan violations.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the productivity KPI and is responsible for achieving it through orchestration of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-001.1 through 001.4) are allocated to Vehicle, Operator Interface, Teleoperation, and Safety Services respectively. Each sub-FR has a clear owner and verification method.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Specify interface latency and bandwidth requirements as part of DP specification. Define operator reaction time budget (e.g., <200 ms for on-site, <500 ms for teleoperation) and map to Network Link and SafetyAlert latency constraints.
Revision 2: Define NGHE Solution module responsibilities explicitly in the IM specification: task scheduling, telemetry aggregation, safety constraint enforcement, productivity metric calculation.
Revision 3: Specify SafetyAlert false-positive rate acceptance criteria (e.g., <1 false positive per 8 operating hours) to prevent operator override behavior.

Revision 4: Define productivity measurement mechanism: what data is logged, how is cycle time calculated, what is the evaluation period, how are safety violations handled (e.g., do they invalidate the measurement)?

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-001 is not atomic; it has been refined into four sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-001.1 through 001.4) that address vehicle execution, operator interface, safety compliance, and teleoperation link quality. Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to identify specific DPs and IMs. The current level (NGHE Solution) is the correct container for the productivity KPI, but the sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level to identify concrete design parameters and implementation modules (e.g., Vehicle subsystem, Operator Interface module, Teleoperation module, Safety Services module). Proceeding to the next level will enable detailed design and allocation of responsibilities.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR is well-formed and the selected DP (modular architecture) is sound. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) has high containment (95/100) and low diffusion (5/100). However, the FR is not atomic; it depends on four sub-FRs that must be decomposed further. Proceeding to ZIGZAG_DOWN allows each sub-FR to be refined, mapped to specific DPs, and allocated to concrete IMs (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, Operator Interface). This will enable detailed design and verification planning.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-001.1: The vehicle shall execute commanded tasks (dig, load, transport, dump) with sufficient speed and precision to achieve target cycle times.
CR-NGHE-001.2: The operator interface (on-site or remote) shall provide sufficient situational awareness and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution.
CR-NGHE-001.3: The NGHE solution shall enforce safety constraints (geofencing, speed limits, collision avoidance) that do not degrade productivity below the target.
CR-NGHE-001.4: The teleoperation system (if deployed) shall maintain communication link quality and latency sufficient to sustain operator control fidelity and task execution rate.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Standardized interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) can be defined with sufficient bandwidth and latency guarantees to support both on-site and remote operation.

Assumption 2: Vehicle hardware (hydraulics, bucket, drivetrain) can achieve target cycle times without modification; productivity is primarily a function of operator effectiveness and task orchestration.
Assumption 3: Operator (on-site or remote) is capable of executing tasks efficiently if provided with adequate situational awareness and control responsiveness.
Assumption 4: Safety constraints can be enforced without degrading productivity below the target (i.e., safety and productivity are not in fundamental conflict).
Assumption 5: Teleoperation link quality (latency, bandwidth, availability) can be maintained at levels sufficient to sustain operator control fidelity.
open_risks: Risk 1: If interface latency exceeds operator reaction time budget, teleoperation productivity will degrade below target.
Risk 2: If safety service integration introduces false-positive alerts, operator will override safety constraints, negating safety compliance.
Risk 3: If Vehicle team optimizes for speed without regard to operator control fidelity, productivity may degrade in practice despite high vehicle performance.
Risk 4: If Teleoperation module is not tightly integrated with NGHE Solution, network latency may not be managed end-to-end.
Risk 5: If productivity measurement mechanism is not rigorous (e.g., does not account for safety violations or downtime), the KPI may not be verifiable or actionable.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Interface latency and bandwidth specifications for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link. Mapping to operator reaction time budget and safety-critical latency requirements.
Evidence 2: Vehicle cycle time data (dig, load, transport, dump) under various soil conditions and load factors. Verification that target cycle times are achievable.

Evidence 3: Operator interface design (on-site and remote) with situational awareness metrics (e.g., visibility, control responsiveness, feedback latency). Verification that operator can execute tasks efficiently.
Evidence 4: Safety constraint specifications (geofencing, speed limits, collision avoidance) with false-positive rate data. Verification that safety constraints do not degrade productivity.

Evidence 5: Teleoperation link quality data (latency, bandwidth, availability) under representative network conditions. Verification that link quality is sufficient to sustain operator control fidelity.
Evidence 6: Productivity measurement mechanism specification and representative operations trial data. Verification that productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period.
